full_notes:
How Do Organisms Reproduce is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that science 128 Figure 8.1 Figure 8.1 Figure 8.1 Figure 8.1 Figure 8.1 Creation of diversity over succeeding generations.

The text explains that the original organism at the top will give rise to, say, two individuals, similar in body design, but with subtle differences.

The text explains that each of them, in turn, will give rise to two individuals in the next generation.

The text explains that each of the four individuals in the bottom row will be different from each other.

The text explains that while some of these differences will be unique, others will be inherited from their respective parents, who were different from each other.

The text explains that heredity 8 CHAPTER We have seen that reproductive processes give rise to new individuals that are similar, but subtly different.

The text explains that we have discussed how some amount of variation is produced even during asexual reproduction.

The text explains that and the number of successful variations are maximised by the process of sexual reproduction.

The text explains that if we observe a field of sugarcane we find very little variations among the individual plants.

The text explains that but in a number of animals including human beings, which reproduce sexually, quite distinct variations are visible among different individuals.

The text explains that in this chapter, we shall be studying the mechanism by which variations are created and inherited.

The text explains that 1 A A A A ACCUMUL CCUMUL CCUMUL CCUMUL CCUMUL A A A A ATION OF V TION OF V TION OF V TION OF V TION OF VARIA ARIA ARIA ARIA ARIATION TION TION TION TION DURING REPRODUCTION DURING REPRODUCTION DURING REPRODUCTION DURING REPRODUCTION DURING REPRODUCTION Inheritance from the previous generation provides both a common basic body design, and subtle changes in it, for the next generation.

The text explains that now think about what would happen when this new generation, in its turn, reproduces.

The text explains that the second generation will have differences that they inherit from the first generation, as well as newly created differences (Fig.

The text explains that figure 8.1 would represent the situation if a single individual reproduces, as happens in asexual reproduction.

The text explains that if one bacterium divides, and then the resultant two bacteria divide again, the four individual bacteria generated would be very similar.

The text explains that heredity 129 8.2 HEREDITY 8.2 HEREDITY 8.2 HEREDITY 8.2 HEREDITY 8.2 HEREDITY The most obvious outcome of the reproductive process still remains the generation of individuals of similar design.

The text explains that the rules of heredity determine the process by which traits and characteristics are reliably inherited.

The text explains that we know that a child bears all the basic features of a human being.

The text explains that bacteria that can withstand heat will survive better in a heat wave, as we have discussed earlier.

The text explains that selection of variants by environmental factors forms the basis for evolutionary processes, as we will discuss in later sections.

The text explains that so the next question was, were the tall plants in the F1 generation exactly the same as the tall plants of the parent generation?

The text explains that cellular DNA is the information source for making proteins in the cell.

The text explains that the amount of the plant hormone made will depend on the efficiency of the process for making it.

The text explains that consider now an enzyme that is important for this process.

The text explains that we have disscussed this issue in the previous Chapter.

The text explains that if progeny plants inherited a single whole gene set from each parent, then the experiment explained in Fig.

The text explains that this is explained by the fact that each gene set is present, not as a single long thread of DNA, but as separate independent pieces, each called a chromosome.

The text explains that such a mechanism of inheritance explains the results of the Mendel experiments, and is used by all sexually reproducing organisms.

The text explains that 8.2.4 Sex Determination We have discussed the idea that the two sexes participating in sexual reproduction must be somewhat different from each other for a number of reasons.

The text explains that thus, in some animals like a few reptiles, the temperature at which fertilised eggs are kept determines whether the animals developing in the eggs will be male or female.

The text explains that what you have learnt n Variations arising during the process of reproduction can be inherited. n These variations may lead to increased survival of the individuals. n Sexually reproducing individuals have two copies of genes for the same trait.

The text explains that if the copies are not identical, the trait that gets expressed is called the dominant trait and the other is called the recessive trait. n Traits in one individual may be inherited separately, giving rise to new combinations of traits in the offspring of sexual reproduction. n Sex is determined by different factors in various species.

The text explains that in human beings, the sex of the child depends on whether the paternal chromosome is X (for girls) or Y (for boys).

The text explains that a Mendelian experiment consisted of breeding tall pea plants bearing violet flowers with short pea plants bearing white flowers.

The text explains that the progeny all bore violet flowers, but almost half of them were short.

The text explains that this suggests that the genetic make-up of the tall parent can be depicted as (a) TTWW (b) TTww (c) TtWW (d) TtWw 2.

The text explains that a study found that children with light-coloured eyes are likely to have parents with light-coloured eyes.

The text explains that on this basis, can we say anything about whether the light eye colour trait is dominant or recessive?

The text explains that outline a project which aims to find the dominant coat colour in dogs.

The text explains that how is the equal genetic contribution of male and female parents ensured in the progeny?

Source: NCERT Class 10 Science, Chapter 8: How Do Organisms Reproduce
